region_of_origin: China (Sichuan origin); widely served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for brown sugar ice jelly (bing fen). Mapped to the closest parent class 'jelly dessert food product' as an approximate match.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for bing fen / brown sugar jelly. A0CKP (sugar confectionery / jelly-type sweet) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps to this traditional Chinese chilled jelly dessert.

main_ingredients: bing fen jelly (fig seed jelly / Physalis alkekengi seed starch)
optional toppings: peanuts, sesame seeds, raisins, hawthorn flakes, osmanthus syrup

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of brown sugar jelly (approximately 250 g) as typically served at Singaporean hawker stalls and dessert shops.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Gelatin dessert, dry mix, prepared with water (SR Legacy) and Brown sugar (SR Legacy); composite estimate for brown sugar jelly dessert
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for brown sugar bing fen exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for plain gelatin/jelly dessert prepared with water (FDC ID 169640) and brown sugar (FDC ID 168833), weighted to reflect the typical composition of bing fen with brown sugar syrup (approximately 70% jelly base, 30% brown sugar syrup by weight). The input record values (energy 88 kcal, protein 1.0 g, fat 2.2 g, carbohydrate 16.8 g, sugar 14.0 g, sodium 6 mg, fibre 0.6 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Fat 2.2 g reflects optional peanut/sesame toppings in the standard serving. Cholesterol 0.0 mg confirmed zero — plant-based dessert with no animal-derived fat components.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened jelly/gelatin dessert and brown sugar GI values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brown sugar bing fen jelly. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) plain sugar-sweetened gelatin/jelly dessert GI ~60–65 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) brown sugar GI ~65 per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables; (3) the dominant carbohydrate source is free sugars (14.0 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (0.6 g/100 g), supporting a medium-to-high GI estimate. GL per 250 g serving = (65 × 16.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 27.3 g, classified as high GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High free sugar content (14.0 g/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (0.6 g/100 g); sodium is low at 6 mg/100 g but sugar-sweetened desserts independently elevate blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dessert due to its high sugar load (14.0 g/100 g); if consumed, request reduced or no brown sugar syrup and limit portion to half a bowl (≤125 g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Free sugar 14.0 g/100 g with only 0.6 g/100 g dietary fibre produces a high glycaemic load (GL ~27 per standard 250 g serving); rapid postprandial glucose spike is expected.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dessert in standard portions; the brown sugar syrup delivers rapidly absorbed free sugars with minimal fibre buffering, causing significant postprandial hyperglycaemia — if consumed at all, limit to a very small portion (≤50 g) with no added syrup and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (14.0 g/100 g) contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction; saturated fat is low (0.32 g/100 g) and sodium is minimal (6 mg/100 g), which are favourable, but the sugar load remains the primary cardiovascular concern.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dessert only occasionally and in small portions (≤100 g), requesting minimal brown sugar syrup to reduce the free sugar contribution to triglyceride elevation and systemic inflammation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Free sugar at 14.0 g/100 g from brown sugar syrup promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; negligible fibre (0.6 g/100 g) provides no anti-inflammatory buffering.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dessert due to its high sugar content driving systemic inflammation; peanut and sesame toppings provide modest anti-inflammatory unsaturated fats but are insufficient to offset the sugar-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Jelly (红糖冰粉, Hóng Táng Bīng Fěn) is a traditional Chinese chilled dessert originating from Sichuan province and now widely popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia. The base jelly — known as bing fen — is made from the starch extracted from the seeds of Physalis alkekengi (Chinese lantern plant), producing a translucent, soft, wobbly jelly with a delicate neutral flavour and a silky, melt-in-the-mouth texture. The jelly is served cold in a bowl and generously drizzled with warm or room-temperature brown sugar syrup, which provides a rich, molasses-like sweetness. Common toppings include crushed peanuts, toasted sesame seeds, raisins, dried hawthorn flakes, osmanthus syrup, and red bean. Nutritionally, a standard 250 g bowl provides approximately 220 kcal, with carbohydrates (primarily free sugars from the brown sugar syrup) as the dominant macronutrient at 16.8 g/100 g, of which 14.0 g/100 g is sugar. Protein is low at 1.0 g/100 g and fat is modest at 2.2 g/100 g (largely from peanut and sesame toppings). Dietary fibre is minimal at 0.6 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) with a high glycaemic load of approximately 27 per standard serving, making this dessert unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The dessert is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is a popular street food and hawker dessert in Singapore, often sold alongside other traditional Chinese cold desserts.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat brown sugar jelly?
How many calories in brown sugar jelly?
Is brown sugar jelly gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for brown sugar bing fen jelly exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy gelatin dessert and brown sugar entries, consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate from approved peer-reviewed sources. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and variability in toppings across vendors.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for brown sugar bing fen jelly is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; the fat content (2.2 g/100 g) primarily reflects peanut and sesame toppings but fatty acid breakdown is not reported for this composite dish. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this traditional composite dessert are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — this is a plant-based dessert with no hydrogenated fat components. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — no animal-derived fat components in the base recipe. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy data for plain jelly dessert and brown sugar; no significant vitamin C source in this dish. The input record's cultural.primary_cuisine field ('Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles 福记红烧牛肉面 - Lorong Telok') appears to be a data entry error (vendor name incorrectly mapped to cuisine field) and has been corrected to 'Chinese / Singaporean Chinese'. Ethnic tags have been corrected accordingly. GI estimated at 65 based on analogous sugar-sweetened jelly dessert and brown sugar GI values from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). GL per 250 g serving = (65 × 16.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 27.3.
